planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted tip-cuttings in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; sawtooth blackberry spreads by root suckers and tip-rooting canes and can colonize disturbed areas aggressively. Best managed by keeping within defined areas — mowing around the perimeter in spring controls spread.

directions: Plant divisions or rooted tip-cuttings in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; sawtooth blackberry is one of the most common and widespread native blackberries of the eastern and southeastern US — a vigorous, thorny-caned shrub with large, deeply sawtooth-toothed compound leaves and masses of white flowers in spring followed by sweet-tart black drupelets in midsummer. One of several eastern blackberry species (along with Rubus allegheniensis and others) forming the backbone of wild blackberry patches in woodlands, old fields, roadsides, and disturbed areas from New England to Florida. The fruit is sweet-tart and excellent for fresh eating, jams, cobblers, and pies. In the natural garden, sawtooth blackberry forms important wildlife thickets — the thorny canes provide nesting sites and escape cover for songbirds, rabbits, and ground-nesting animals; the berries feed more than 100 species of birds and mammals. More tolerant of partial shade and moist soils than many other native blackberries.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted tip-cuttings in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, fertile to average, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; California blackberry grows naturally in moist coastal and foothill habitats — streambanks, woodland edges, and shaded ravines along the Pacific Coast from British Columbia to southern California. More shade and moisture tolerant than many blackberries.

directions: Plant divisions or rooted tip-cuttings in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, fertile to average, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; California blackberry is the native blackberry of the Pacific Coast — and one of the most historically significant berry plants in horticultural history, because it is one of the parent species of some of the world's most important hybrid blackberry cultivars. Rubus ursinus is a parent of the boysenberry (R. ursinus × loganberry), the loganberry (R. ursinus × red raspberry), and the marionberry (a complex hybrid involving R. ursinus) — three of the Pacific Northwest's most important blackberry cultivars. The small, intensely flavorful native blackberries (smaller than commercial blackberries but more aromatic and complex in flavor) are highly prized for fresh eating and making jams, pies, and wine. The plant is a sprawling, slender-caned, moderately thorny shrub that tolerates considerable shade — growing naturally in the moist shade of Douglas-fir forests and redwood groves along the Pacific Coast. The deeply lobed, compound leaves are attractive. An excellent native for wildlife gardens, edible landscapes, and shaded moist woodland edges in the Pacific Coast states.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, rocky or loamy soil in full sun; boulder raspberry grows naturally on rocky slopes, canyon walls, and foothills of the Colorado Front Range and Rocky Mountains. More drought-tolerant than fruiting raspberries and entirely thornless.

directions: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, rocky or loamy soil in full sun; boulder raspberry is one of the most beautiful ornamental native shrubs of the Rocky Mountain foothills — an entirely thornless, arching shrub with attractive, deeply lobed, maplelike leaves and stunning large (up to 2.5 inch), pure white, rose-like flowers in late spring. The species name 'deliciosus' was reportedly given because the flowers are so delightful, though the fruit (small, dry, sparse drupelets) is technically edible but far from impressive compared to other raspberries. The magnificent white flowers in May and June make boulder raspberry outstanding as a flowering ornamental in dry, rocky garden situations in zones 4–8. Completely thornless — unlike virtually all other Rubus — making it safe for high-traffic areas of the garden. The state flower of Colorado (not officially, but very emblematic of the Front Range foothills). Drought tolerant once established, appropriate for xeriscape plantings in the Rocky Mountain West.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in well-drained, strongly acidic, cool, humus-rich, coniferous duff or peaty soil in full shade; speckled wood lily grows naturally under the deep shade of conifer forests in the Pacific Northwest and Sierra Nevada, requiring cool temperatures, consistently moist (but not wet) acidic, humus-rich soil, and low light. Difficult to establish outside these conditions.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in well-drained, strongly acidic, cool, humus-rich, coniferous duff or peaty soil in full shade; speckled wood lily is a tiny, enchanting woodland native of Pacific Coast and Rocky Mountain conifer forests — the western counterpart of the eastern yellow clintonia (Clintonia borealis), but producing a single, upward-facing, white flower (rather than multiple yellow flowers) above a small rosette of large, shiny, oval basal leaves. The single white flower with its delicate speckles is borne on a short stem and is followed by a striking, metallic blue-purple berry in late summer. The blue berries are distinctive and beautiful but inedible. Found in cool, moist, deeply shaded Douglas-fir, hemlock, and red fir forests from southern Alaska through British Columbia and cascading through the Sierra Nevada. Requires essentially the same conditions as its habitat: deep conifer shade, consistently cool temperatures, and acidic, humus-rich soil with good moisture retention. A gem for conifer shade gardens in the Pacific Northwest and mountain climates.

planting.note: Plant tubers or divisions in spring in well-drained to moist, strongly acidic to slightly acidic, cool, humus-rich, loamy woodland soil in full to partial shade; dwarf ginseng grows naturally in moist, cool eastern deciduous forests and requires consistently moist, shaded, acidic, humus-rich conditions. Very slow to establish from seed — division of the small tuber-like root is more practical.

directions: Plant tubers or divisions in spring in well-drained to moist, strongly acidic to slightly acidic, cool, humus-rich, loamy woodland soil in full to partial shade; dwarf ginseng is the smallest and least-known member of the ginseng family (Araliaceae) — a tiny, ephemeral woodland wildflower of cool, moist eastern deciduous forests from Nova Scotia and Quebec south to the Appalachians. Unlike its much larger relative American ginseng (Panax quinquefolius), dwarf ginseng produces a small, round, tuber-like root (rather than the large, spindle-shaped ginseng root). The small plant emerges early in spring, produces a tiny umbel of white flowers, sets seed, and dies back to the ground by midsummer — a spring ephemeral that completes its entire above-ground life cycle in just a few weeks. The small, peppery-tasting root was eaten raw by Cherokee, Iroquois, and other Indigenous peoples. An excellent, highly ephemeral native wildflower for naturalizing in deeply shaded, moist, acidic woodland gardens where it grows with hepatica, bloodroot, trout lily, and spring beauty.
